titleStmt.title: Letter from F. J. Cribb, 6 August 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: F. J. Cribb

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from F.J. Cribb, chief engineer with Marshall's. It is not clear to whom this letter is addressed although the matter was dealt with in the chief secretary's office. In this letter, Cribb asks whether or not he will require a permit in order to visit Ireland. During his visit , Cribb intends to spend a fortnight fishing on Lough Corrib as he has done for the past four years. He describes himself as English, born in London, and mentions his work with Marshall's. A note added to the letter (in the CSO) indicates that British subjects do not require a permit to visit Ireland.The office of chief secretary for Ireland was vacated by Augustine Birrell (1850-1933) on 30 April, 1916. Birrell and his under-secretary Sir. Matthew Nathan had offered their resignations in the aftermath of the Easter Rising of 1916. Henry Edward Duke (1855-1939), subsequently took up the position of chief secretary on 31 July 1916.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers, CSO RP/1916/5620/13589

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Sand Rock House Retford Aug 6 th 16 Dear Sir I shall be much obliged if you
will kindly inform me whether
a 'Permit' is still necessary to
visit Ireland &if it is to give me
the address of the official to apply to
for one.
I want to come over as I have done for
the past 4 years for a fortnights
fishing in Lough Corrib staying at
Oughterard. I am English born in
London & and am Chief Engineer to
Mess rs Marshall & Co Garrisboro
with whom I have been for 28 years Thanking you in anticipation
& enclosing stamped envelope I remain
Yrs. faithfully
F J Cribb Inform that British subjects
do not require a permit to
visit Ireland
8 - 8 - 16
Done 10/8
despd 10/8
